comment_content: As someone who has edited many documentaries, this is the most one-sided documentary I've ever seen. There were over 270 contestants on the show, and we're seeing the same 5 people tell you how awful it was. Plus, Joelle, who's own teammate and best friend, isn't in the documentary, and she was upset with Joelle for not giving it her all. Now Joelle blames it on racism. People magazine looked into the 18 winners, and more than half said it was a positive life-saving experience and have kept most of the weight off. But if you watch the documentary, you'd think that it was just evil Bob and Jillian screaming at everyone for an hour a night. There is no balance to the documentary. If People magazine could find people who found it a positive experience, why do we not see even one person on the screen with that message? It's because the producer's goal is to skewer the show and the people who created it. The one guy who is on the show, who shows off how big he is and says, "Clearly, you can see it didn't work for me." Is that the show's fault or the fact that you didn't continue to eat well or exercise? You don't have to exercise like they do on the show to maintain your weight. But apparently it's the show's fault. I have seen others on podcasts who didn't win but were in the finals who have mostly maintained their weight and felt it was a life-changing experience. Why is that not represented in the documentary??? Because the producers had an agenda. Let me point out that I'm not saying that everything the show did was a good idea, and unfortunately, TV producers will always push the envelope for ratings, which is why the doctor should have pushed back more, but let's not make the entire idea evil and 100% bad. There were positives and negatives based on what I've read from former contestants, but the producers of this doc decided to only show the negatives.

reply_content: ⁠​⁠​⁠@John_Hooper_is_backThere are so many variables that go into losing weight, it’s impossible to generalize for everyone.  Everything I’ve learned tells me losing 80 pounds in a very short period of time is NOT good for you.  Did you watch the show?  They did not give their metabolism time to adjust.  If you go from 800-1,000 calories/day back to a (normal) 2,000 calories/day, AND discontinue training 6-8 hours/day, you are GOING to gain weight back.  It’s also very hard on your heart.  Any sensible nutritionist, let alone a doctor, will tell you that.  To boot, these trainers never worked with morbidly obese clients before.  The doctor on the show pointed out that the risk of injury goes up exponentially the higher the scale goes.  In my opinion the trainers couldn’t possibly know all the ramifications of what they were enforcing.  I’m not saying they did it intentionally, but they should have been more flexible with their one-workout-fits-all mentality.

reply_content: Actually, there is a strong correlation between maintaining a healthy body weight and better health outcomes—especially when it comes to reducing the risk of chronic diseases. Numerous large-scale studies show that excess body fat—especially visceral fat—is strongly associated with increased risks for heart disease, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, stroke, sleep apnea, and even some cancers. The risk increases as BMI moves into the overweight and obese ranges. Even modest weight loss in people with obesity has been shown to significantly improve metabolic markers like blood pressure, blood sugar, and cholesterol levels.

comment_content:
There's a lot of debate around The Biggest Loser and how it portrayed weight loss, but the real crisis that often gets overlooked is the massive and deadly impact of obesity itself. In the U.S., over 42% of adults are classified as obese. That’s not just a cosmetic issue—it’s a public health emergency.

Obesity is linked to over 230 diseases, including heart disease, type 2 diabetes, stroke, and at least 13 types of cancer. It leads to more than 300,000 premature deaths every year in the U.S. alone. On average, it can shorten a person's life by up to 10 years.

And then there’s the cost. Obesity-related healthcare costs in the U.S. are estimated at $173 billion annually. Individuals with obesity spend over $1,800 more per year on medical expenses compared to those at a healthy weight.

We need to stop sugarcoating this issue. It's not about fat-shaming—it's about confronting a preventable epidemic that’s silently killing millions and bankrupting our healthcare system. Prevention, education, and access to healthier food and lifestyles need to be national priorities.

comment_content:
There's a lot of debate around The Biggest Loser and how it portrayed weight loss, but the real crisis that often gets overlooked is the massive and deadly impact of obesity itself. In the U.S., over 42% of adults are classified as obese. That’s not just a cosmetic issue—it’s a public health emergency.

Obesity is linked to over 230 diseases, including heart disease, type 2 diabetes, stroke, and at least 13 types of cancer. It leads to more than 300,000 premature deaths every year in the U.S. alone. On average, it can shorten a person's life by up to 10 years.

And then there’s the cost. Obesity-related healthcare costs in the U.S. are estimated at $173 billion annually. Individuals with obesity spend over $1,800 more per year on medical expenses compared to those at a healthy weight.

We need to stop sugarcoating this issue. It's not about fat-shaming—it's about confronting a preventable epidemic that’s silently killing millions and bankrupting our healthcare system. Prevention, education, and access to healthier food and lifestyles need to be national priorities.

If we see people who are sickly skinny (famine skinny) our brains are flooded with signals telling us that this person is unhealthy and damaged.
